title: Mortise and tenon oak picture frame
subtitle: A hand-jointed picture frame in seasoned oak with through-wedged tenon corners
excerpt: A picture frame with mortise and tenon corners is stronger and more repairable than a mitred frame and shows the joint as part of the design. Oak is the right species: strong, fine-grained, and characteristic of traditional British joined woodwork. The through-wedged tenon version uses no glue; the wedges can be knocked out if the frame ever needs disassembly.
sourceNotes: Mortise and tenon joinery documented in Moxon's Mechanick Exercises (1703) and throughout British furniture and joinery traditions; through-wedged tenon frames recorded in museum collections of historic woodwork.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rectangular slot cut into one timber to receive the tenon of another. The mortise on a picture frame stile is cut through the full width of the rail, leaving a slot into which the tenon from the adjacent rail passes.

The reduced end of a timber that passes into the mortise of the adjacent piece. The tenon on each rail has its width and thickness reduced to match the mortise dimensions, leaving two shoulders that seat against the mortise piece face.
Air-dried or kiln-dried timber at stable moisture content. Stable oak is essential for a frame: any movement after joinery will open gaps at the shoulders.

A thin tapered piece of hardwood driven into a saw kerf in the tenon end, within the mortise, to lock the joint by expanding the tenon inside the slot. A through-wedged joint requires no glue and can be disassembled by knocking the wedge back.

projectSchedule.title: Cut and fit all joints
projectSchedule.body: Mark and cut mortises and tenons on all four corners. Dry-fit, check squareness, drive wedges. A full day.

projectSchedule.title: Sand and oil
projectSchedule.body: Sand to 240 grit, apply raw linseed oil.

projectSchedule.title: Oil cured: ready to hang
projectSchedule.body: Two weeks after oiling the frame is ready for use.

content.text: A mortise and
tenon
frame uses the same joint that has held oak furniture together for five hundred years. Each corner has a
mortise
in the stile (vertical) and a tenon on the rail (horizontal); the tenon passes through the mortise and is locked with
wedges
Seasoned
oak is traditional; the visible end grain of the wedged tenon on the front face of the frame is a feature, not a problem.
What you need: wood

Seasoned oak rails, 30 mm wide and 18 mm thick, in lengths to suit the frame size. For an A4-sized frame opening (21 cm x 30 cm), the outer frame dimensions are approximately 27 cm x 36 cm (allowing for 3 cm frame width on all sides). Cut two stiles at 40 cm and two rails at 33 cm (both with tenon length included). The tenon length equals the stile width (30 mm) plus 5 mm protrusion for wedging.
What you need: tools

A tenon saw for cutting tenon cheeks and shoulders. A bench chisel (8 to 10 mm width) for chopping and paring the mortise. A carpenter's mallet. A marking gauge set to the tenon and mortise dimensions. A marking knife for shoulder lines. Sandpaper in 120, 180, and 240 grit. Raw linseed oil.
The work
Step 1: Mark and cut the tenons

The tenon on each rail end is centred on the rail thickness with equal cheeks. For an 18 mm thick rail, a centred tenon of 8 mm thickness leaves 5 mm cheeks on each face. Set the marking gauge and scribe the tenon thickness on the end grain and both faces. Scribe the shoulder line (at the frame rabbet depth). Saw the two cheeks with the tenon saw on the waste side, then saw the shoulders. Remove the cheek waste; the tenon is now visible.
Step 2: Chop the mortises

Mark the mortise on the stile face: its width equals the tenon width (the full frame rail width minus any edge rebates), and its length equals the tenon thickness (8 mm). Chop the mortise with the bench chisel and mallet, working from the centre outward. Oak requires firm mallet blows; work in stages, clearing waste with each pass. The mortise must be through the full width of the stile (30 mm) so the tenon can protrude for wedging.
Step 3: Test fit

Push the tenon into the mortise by hand. It should enter with firm hand pressure and the shoulders should seat flush against the stile face with no rocking. Any high spot on the mortise walls shows as a burnish on the tenon; pare the mortise at that point. The fit is correct when the shoulder lines are closed and the joint is snug.
Step 4: Cut saw kerfs for wedges

With the tenon fitted (not yet glued or wedged), saw two kerfs into the protruding tenon end from the front, parallel to the tenon width, each 8 mm in from the tenon edges. The kerfs are 20 to 25 mm deep. These slots receive the wedges; when driven, the wedges expand the tenon inside the mortise and lock the joint.
Step 5: Assemble and wedge

Dry-fit all four corners, check squareness with a try square, then disassemble. Apply a small amount of PVA to the shoulders only (not the tenon cheeks, to allow for disassembly if needed). Re-assemble, drive the wedges with a mallet until they are tight. Check squareness immediately and hold until the glue sets (four hours minimum). Trim the protruding tenon and wedge flush to the stile face with a chisel after setting.
Finishing

Sand through 120, 180, and 240 grit. Oak sands to a very fine surface at 240 grit. Apply raw linseed oil to all surfaces and allow to soak. Wipe off. Allow two weeks for the oil to set. Oak darkens pleasantly with each oiling.
Variations
Use ash in place of oak: ash is lighter in colour, equally strong, and the through-wedged tenon is equally visible on the front face.
Make a square frame (30 cm x 30 cm) for a print or mirror: the same joint at each corner, simpler to lay out because all four members are the same length.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems with mortise and tenon fit and frame squareness.

items.symptom: The shoulder has a gap on one face when the joint is assembled
items.cause: The tenon shoulder was not sawn square to the rail length; it leans in one direction.
items.fix: Pare the shoulder back to square with a wide chisel, working across the grain very lightly. Test against a try square after each pass.

items.symptom: The frame is out of square after wedging
items.cause: One tenon was fitted more tightly than the others, or a clamp pulled diagonally during setting.
items.fix: Measure the diagonals immediately after assembly. A wedge-and-clamp adjustment while glue is wet can correct up to 2 mm of out-of-square. Beyond that, the joints need to be re-fitted.

items.symptom: The oak mortise chops ragged rather than clean
items.cause: The chisel is dull, or the oak has interlocked grain at this point.
items.fix: Hone the chisel. Chop mortises in oak in stages, clearing waste frequently rather than trying to achieve final depth in one pass. Work from both faces to meet in the middle.
